settled.decision: All five reports carve this concept in essentially identical terms — a licensed or skilled-trade adult who offers to reduce contractor costs. R0's `physical_trade_skill`, R1's `hands_on_trade_skills`, R2's `hands_on_trade_skill`, R3's `hands_on_trade_skill`, and R4's `physical_maintenance_skills` would all score the same applicant the same way. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Hands-On Trade / Physical Maintenance Skill

settled.decision: R0's `financial_admin_skill`, R1's `financial_governance_skills`, R2's `financial_governance_skill`, R3's `financial_governance_skill`, and the financial-administration component of R4's `governance_and_financial_admin_skills` all measure whether a household can manage co-op finances. They would score the same applicant identically. Merged. The meeting/chairing component of R4's `governance_and_financial_admin_skills` is absorbed into the `formal_board_governance_experience` axis instead.
settled.name: Financial Governance Skill

settled.decision: R0's `co_op_governance_depth`, R1's `board_governance_skills`, R2's `governance_meeting_experience`, R3's `meeting_and_governance_experience`, and R4's `formal_governance_role_experience` (plus the meeting-governance component of R4's `governance_and_financial_admin_skills`) all measure formal governance role experience. All would score a veteran strata chair identically high and a governance newcomer identically low. Note: financial skill is kept separate (own axis) because a household can be a skilled treasurer but poor at chairing meetings, and vice versa. Merged into one axis here.
settled.name: Formal Board / Meeting Governance Experience

settled.decision: R1's `housing_coop_experience_depth`, R2's `depth_of_coop_collective_experience`, R3's `co_op_specific_experience_depth`, and R4's `depth_of_cooperative_living_experience` all specifically measure lived housing co-op experience, and deliberately distinguish it from general governance experience (which is measured separately). A household could hold formal board roles (scoring high on `formal_board_governance_experience`) while never having lived in a housing co-op (scoring low here), proving these are distinct axes. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Housing Co-op / Co-housing Living Experience

settled.decision: R0's `people_and_conflict_skill`, R1's `conflict_resolution_skills`, R2's `conflict_mediation_skill`, R3's `conflict_and_mediation_skill`, and the mediation component of R4's `care_mediation_and_social_skills` all measure professional or practised skill at resolving others' disputes. This is kept distinct from `collaborative_disposition` (how well the applicant themselves behaves in governance) — a skilled mediator could still be combative, and a collaborative person need not be a trained mediator. The R4 axis bundles mediation with health care and social programming; those components are split to their respective axes.
settled.name: Conflict Mediation Skill

settled.decision: R0's `medical_first_aid_readiness`, R1's `community_health_safety_skills`, R2's `health_safety_skill`, R3's `health_and_safety_skill`, and the healthcare component of R4's `care_mediation_and_social_skills` all measure the same specific capacity: a healthcare professional offering first-aid and safety services to co-op members. Kept distinct from conflict mediation and community programming because a nurse is not a mediator and a teacher is not a nurse. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Health & First-Aid Safety Skill

settled.decision: R0's `digital_systems_skill`, R1's `technology_systems_skills`, R2's `digital_systems_skill`, R3's `digital_and_systems_skill`, and R4's `digital_and_systems_infrastructure_skills` all measure the same specific technical capacity. All five definitions converge on a software developer building a co-op management system. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Digital / Systems Infrastructure Skill

settled.decision: R0's `community_programme_skill`, R1's `community_programming_skills`, R2's `programmatic_contribution_for_children` and `kitchen_and_food_contribution`, R3's `social_programming_contribution`, and the programming component of R4's `care_mediation_and_social_skills` all measure whether a household can generate structured member-benefit programming. R2's `kitchen_and_food_contribution` and `programmatic_contribution_for_children` are sub-types of this concept — a professional chef running shared meals and a teacher running a homework club both score high on the same underlying axis. An applicant skilled at running youth programmes but not cooking would still score high here, confirming this is one axis. Merged into one.
settled.name: Community Programming & Education Skill

settled.decision: R2's `community_service_professional_skill` and the social-work/interpretation component of R4's `care_mediation_and_social_skills` both measure a professional capacity to connect community members to external support systems. Kept distinct from `community_programming_skill` (structured programming like homework clubs and cooking) and from `conflict_mediation_skill` (mediating interpersonal disputes): a social worker connecting people to city services is not the same as running a homework club or mediating a neighbour dispute.
settled.name: Community-Service / Social-Support Professional Skill

settled.decision: R0's `outdoor_grounds_skill`, R1's `outdoor_grounds_skills`, R2's `grounds_and_outdoor_skill`, and R3's `physical_grounds_and_outdoor_skill` all measure the same specific capacity: a gardener or arborist offering to manage shared outdoor spaces. R4 does not have a separate grounds axis; that contribution domain appears in R4's `breadth_of_contribution_roles` as one listed domain. Kept distinct from `hands_on_trade_skill` (indoor building trades) — an arborist who gardens scores high here but may have no electrical or masonry skill. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Grounds / Horticultural Skill

settled.decision: R0's `maintenance_ops_admin_experience`, R1's `estate_administration_skills`, R2's `maintenance_coordination_skill`, and R3's `maintenance_coordination_skill` all measure the administrative side of maintenance (scheduling, quotes, warranties) as explicitly distinct from doing the physical work. R4 does not have a separate axis for this; the concept is subsumed within R4's `formal_governance_role_experience` or `breadth_of_contribution_roles`. Kept distinct from `hands_on_trade_skill` — a household could be expert at scheduling contractors without being a tradesperson, and vice versa. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Maintenance Coordination & Contractor Administration Skill

settled.decision: R0's `new_member_onboarding_experience`, R1's `member_onboarding_capacity`, R2's `member_onboarding_skill`, and R3's `member_onboarding_and_mentorship_skill` all measure the same operational function: running orientations and keeping volunteers engaged. Kept distinct from `knowledge_transfer_orientation` (documenting systems so knowledge persists) — a household can be great at welcoming new members without ever writing anything down, and vice versa. R4 does not have a dedicated axis for this; onboarding is listed as one domain within `breadth_of_contribution_roles`. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: New-Member Onboarding & Volunteer Engagement Skill

settled.decision: R0's `knowledge_transfer_orientation`, R1's `knowledge_transfer_ethic`, R3's `peer_network_skills_transfer`, and R4's `documentation_and_knowledge_transfer` all measure the same behavioural orientation: writing things down and mentoring successors. R2 does not have a dedicated axis for this concept. Kept distinct from `member_onboarding_skill` (welcoming new members) — one can mentor a successor in a specific role without running general orientation, and vice versa. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Knowledge-Transfer & Documentation Orientation

settled.decision: R0's `stated_participation_commitment`, R1's `participation_commitment`, R2's `participation_commitment`, R3's `participation_commitment`, and R4's `explicit_work_hour_commitment` all measure the same thing: how explicitly and strongly the applicant commits to showing up. R4's axis is a narrower carving (work hours and AGM specifically) but would score the same applicants the same way as the broader versions — an applicant who commits to everything scores high; one who opts out scores low. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Stated Commitment to Active Co-op Participation

settled.decision: R0's `long_term_stay_intent`, R1's `long_term_residency_intent`, R2's `long_term_residency_intent`, R3's `long_term_residency_intent`, and R4's `long_term_residency_intent` are identical in concept and would score the same applicants identically. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Long-Term Residency Intent

settled.decision: R0's `genuine_cooperative_ethos`, R1's `motivation_alignment`, R2's `cooperative_philosophy_alignment`, R3's `cooperative_philosophy_alignment`, and R4's `cooperative_values_alignment` all measure the same construct: values-driven vs. economically-driven motivation for co-op membership. All would score a committed mutualist high and a purely rent-driven applicant low. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Cooperative Philosophy Alignment

settled.decision: R0's `interpersonal_approachability` (informal warmth with neighbours, hosting, sharing food), R2's `community_integration_orientation` (desire to form genuine relationships within the co-op), and R4's `communal_vs_private_household_orientation` (communal vs. private home-life framing) all measure the same underlying axis: how communally versus privately the household orients itself at home. A plausible applicant who is very warm interpersonally but is explicit about wanting privacy would score differently, but the definitions would all catch that in the same direction. These are re-carvings of one concept. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Communal / Social Orientation Toward Neighbours

settled.decision: R0's `community_engagement_outside_coop`, R1's `neighbour_social_integration`, R3's `community_outreach_orientation`, and R4's `outside_community_engagement` all measure the same thing: how actively the household participates in structured external community activities. Kept distinct from `communal_social_orientation` (how communally vs. privately one orients at home) — a household can be deeply involved in external civic life while preferring privacy at home, and vice versa. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Civic & Community Engagement Outside the Co-op

settled.decision: R1's `repair_cafe_diy_culture` (habitual repair, reuse, material stewardship) and R4's `environmental_stewardship_orientation` (composting, growing food, waste reduction, bicycle restoration) are not distinguishable by a separating applicant — someone who volunteers at repair cafés and restores bikes almost invariably also composts and grows food. The two reports are carving the same underlying habitual sustainability orientation from slightly different angles. Merged into one axis. Kept distinct from `hands_on_trade_skill` (professional trade licences) and `outdoor_grounds_skill` (horticultural expertise for shared spaces).
settled.name: Environmental Stewardship & Repair-Reuse Orientation

settled.decision: R0's `conflict_with_governance`, R1's `collaborative_disposition`, R2's `conflict_and_consensus_orientation`, R3's `collaborative_disposition`, and R4's `collaborative_conflict_disposition` all measure the same axis: how constructively the applicant themselves behaves within collective governance (not how skilled they are at mediating others' disputes, which is `conflict_mediation_skill`). A combative person with professional mediation credentials scores low here and high on the mediation skill axis — confirming these are genuinely distinct. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Collaborative Disposition in Shared Governance

settled.decision: R0's `familiarity_with_this_coop`, R1's `existing_coop_connection`, R2's `social_connection_to_this_coop`, R3's `existing_connection_to_this_coop`, and R4's `familiarity_with_this_co_op` all measure the same specific thing: a pre-existing personal connection to this co-op specifically (not co-ops in general). All would score a household with a long-standing member friend identically high. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Prior Familiarity with This Specific Co-op

settled.decision: R0's `available_spare_time_capacity`, R1's `available_discretionary_time`, R2's `available_discretionary_time`, R3's `available_discretionary_time`, and R4's `stated_time_availability` all measure the same self-reported availability for co-op obligations. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Available Discretionary Time for Co-op Work

settled.decision: R0's `income_dual_earner_balance`, R1's `income_balance`, R2's `income_earner_diversification`, R3's `income_concentration_risk`, and R4's `income_earner_redundancy` all measure the same thing: how evenly split the household income is, and therefore how resilient the household is to losing one income. All would score a 50/50 household identically high and a 90/10 household identically low. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Income Earner Balance Between Adults

settled.decision: R0's `applicant_employment_tenure` and `co_applicant_employment_tenure` (two axes, splitting the primary and co-applicant), R1's `employment_tenure_stability`, R2's `employment_tenure_stability`, R3's `income_stability`, and R4's `employment_tenure_of_weaker_earner` all measure how long household members have been in stable employment. R0's split into two separate axes is the finest carving; however, all other reports treat this as a joint household concept, and R4 focuses specifically on the weaker earner. The joint approach is supported by four of five reports and is the appropriate granularity — the committee cares about household-level resilience, and the weaker earner is the binding constraint. R0's two source keys are both absorbed here. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Employment Tenure Stability

settled.decision: R0's `budget_to_housing_charge_headroom`, R1's `financial_headroom`, and R4's `household_income_adequacy` all measure the absolute income buffer above housing costs. Kept distinct from `income_earner_balance` (distribution of income between adults) and from `income_per_household_member` (per-capita adequacy). Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Financial Headroom Above Housing Charge

settled.decision: R4's `income_to_household_size_ratio` is a genuinely distinct axis from `financial_headroom`. A household with $130k and six people has the same absolute income as one with $130k and two people, but radically different per-capita margin. No other report separately calls this out, but it is a real and differentiating dimension of this pool. Kept as a distinct settled axis, absorbing R4's sole source key.
settled.name: Income per Household Member

settled.decision: R0's `essay_specificity_and_groundedness` and R3's `essay_specificity_and_self_knowledge` both measure how concretely and specifically applicants write about themselves — same axis, same scoring. No other report has a dedicated axis for this, but these two align perfectly. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Essay Specificity & Concrete Grounding

settled.decision: R0's `child_age_profile`, R1's `child_age_profile`, R2's `child_independence_level`, R3's `child_age_profile_community_contribution`, and R4's `older_child_community_contribution_potential` all measure the same axis: the age distribution of children and its implications for adult availability and community contribution potential. All would score a family with teenagers identically high and a family with toddlers identically low. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Child Age Profile (School-Age vs. Pre-School)

settled.decision: R1's `household_density`, R2's `household_size_and_space_demand`, and R3's `household_size_to_unit_ratio` all measure the same thing: total household occupancy and its implications for shared-space demand. R4 does not have a dedicated axis for this (household size is used as an input to `income_to_household_size_ratio` but is not separately called out). Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Household Size

settled.decision: R2's `pet_load` and R3's `pet_ownership` both measure the same thing: the number and type of pets and their impact on the co-op community. R0, R1, and R4 do not have dedicated axes for this. Merged into one axis.
settled.name: Pet Load

settled.decision: R4's `breadth_of_contribution_roles` is a meta-axis measuring coverage across multiple domains, which is genuinely distinct from any individual skill axis. A household could score moderately on several individual skill axes while still scoring high on breadth, and a specialist household could score very high on one axis but low on breadth. Kept as a distinct axis. No other report has an exact equivalent, though R4's axis explicitly subsumes onboarding and other domains as examples. Retained with R4's sole source key.
settled.name: Breadth of Co-op Contribution Roles Offered

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the household can perform licensed or practised physical trade work — electrical, mechanical, masonry, plumbing, upholstery, arboriculture — directly applicable to building upkeep, reducing contractor costs.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult holds a trade licence or has years of practised hands-on skill directly applicable to building maintenance (e.g., licensed electrician, tiler/mason, transit mechanic, arborist) and explicitly offers to apply it to co-op upkeep.
dimensions.low_end: Neither adult claims any hands-on trade or maintenance skill; the household explicitly states it would need to pay a professional for any physical repair task.
dimensions.name: Hands-On Trade & Maintenance Skills

dimensions.definition: The household's demonstrated ability to manage co-op finances — budgeting, bookkeeping, account reconciliation, audit preparation, and formal financial reporting — in a governance role.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult has served as co-op or organisational treasurer, passed clean audits, or manages business bookkeeping professionally, and offers to perform these functions for the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: No financial or accounting background is mentioned and no offer to contribute in this area is made.
dimensions.name: Financial Governance & Bookkeeping Skill

dimensions.definition: The household's capacity to chair meetings, manage agendas, keep minutes, conduct elections, and maintain the procedural machinery of a collectively governed body.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult has chaired boards or co-op meetings across multiple contexts, writes clean minutes, runs elections fairly, and can keep long agendas on track.
dimensions.low_end: No meeting facilitation or governance chairmanship experience is mentioned, or the applicant explicitly states disinterest in attending or running meetings.
dimensions.name: Meeting & Board Governance Facilitation Skill

dimensions.definition: The depth and directness of the household's prior experience living in or formally governing a housing co-op or co-housing community specifically — as distinct from analogous experience in strata councils or food co-ops.
dimensions.high_end: Grew up in a housing co-op, lived in one for multiple years and rotated through maintenance and finance teams, or served in a formal co-op governance role (board, finance, membership committee) with demonstrated outcomes.
dimensions.low_end: No prior housing co-op or co-housing experience of any kind; this application would be the household's first exposure.
dimensions.name: Housing Co-op Experience Depth

dimensions.definition: The household's professional or demonstrated ability to de-escalate neighbour disputes, facilitate fair resolution of interpersonal friction, and keep volunteer cohorts functional — distinct from the personal collaborative style measured by collaborative_disposition.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult has formal mediation training, a decade of HR/conflict-resolution practice, or a documented track record of successfully mediating neighbour or member disputes in a collective-living context.
dimensions.low_end: No mediation or dispute-resolution background is offered; or the applicant's own account describes a pattern of clashing with governance bodies rather than resolving friction.
dimensions.name: Conflict Mediation & Dispute Resolution Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether the household includes a healthcare professional able to contribute first-aid resources, CPR training, or emergency health support directly to co-op members.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a registered nurse or equivalent healthcare professional who explicitly offers to set up a first-aid station, run CPR training, or provide health-related resources to members.
dimensions.low_end: No health or safety professional background is offered and no health-related contribution is mentioned.
dimensions.name: Health & Safety Professional Capacity

dimensions.definition: The household's ability to build or maintain digital tools for the co-op — booking systems, maintenance-request trackers, member databases — reducing administrative burden.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a professional software developer or systems administrator who explicitly offers to build and maintain co-op digital infrastructure.
dimensions.low_end: No technical or digital skill applicable to co-op administration is offered.
dimensions.name: Digital & Systems Administration Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether at least one adult has professional or practised skills — teaching, youth work, cooking, facilitation — that they would apply to running structured programmes or shared activities benefiting co-op members and families beyond their own household.
dimensions.high_end: An adult is a qualified teacher, youth worker, or chef who names a specific programme they would run for co-op members (homework club, kids' activities, shared-meal coordination, repair-café facilitation, coding classes) and frames this as part of their co-op contribution.
dimensions.low_end: No programming or instructional skill is offered; the household's contribution is entirely physical, financial, or administrative with no planned community activities.
dimensions.name: Community Programming & Education Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether the household can help co-op members connect with external social services, city benefits, or support networks — skills drawn from social work or community advocacy.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a professional social worker, trained interpreter, or equivalent who explicitly offers to connect neighbours with city services, benefits, or support networks they qualify for.
dimensions.low_end: No social services or community navigation professional background is offered.
dimensions.name: Social Services & Community Navigation Skill

dimensions.definition: The household's capacity to plan, maintain, and steward shared outdoor spaces — gardens, composting, food plots, tree health, grounds safety, seasonal clearance — reducing reliance on paid landscaping.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult is a professional arborist, year-round gardener, or holds equivalent outdoor expertise and explicitly offers to take on shared beds, composting, pruning, or storm cleanup for the co-op.
dimensions.low_end: Neither adult mentions any gardening, horticulture, grounds, or outdoor maintenance skill or interest.
dimensions.name: Outdoor & Grounds Maintenance Skills

dimensions.definition: The household's ability to manage the administrative side of building upkeep — scheduling and vetting tradespeople, obtaining quotes, tracking repairs and warranties, and closing maintenance loops — as distinct from performing the physical work.
dimensions.high_end: At least one adult has chaired a maintenance committee or managed a building repair programme: getting quotes, scheduling contractors, tracking warranties and repairs through to completion.
dimensions.low_end: No experience scheduling trades, obtaining quotes, or managing maintenance records is mentioned; any maintenance involvement is purely physical rather than administrative.
dimensions.name: Maintenance Scheduling & Contractor Coordination Skill

dimensions.definition: Whether the household has experience and willingness to welcome and orient new co-op members — running orientation programmes, managing work-shift scheduling, and supporting early integration.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant has directly run new-member orientation and work-shift scheduling in a co-op or equivalent setting (e.g., food co-op membership committee) and explicitly volunteers to do so again.
dimensions.low_end: No orientation or onboarding experience is mentioned and no offer to assist with new-member integration is made.
dimensions.name: Member Orientation & Onboarding Experience

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant demonstrates awareness of institutional memory loss in volunteer organisations and expresses commitment to documenting processes and mentoring successors so knowledge does not leave with them.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant explicitly describes mentoring their replacement, writing things down for the next volunteer, or keeping systems documented — and has done so in practice in a prior role.
dimensions.low_end: No mention of documentation or knowledge transfer; involvement is described purely in terms of personal tasks completed, with no reference to continuity or succession.
dimensions.name: Knowledge Transfer & Documentation Disposition

dimensions.definition: The strength and specificity of the applicant's expressed willingness to show up for work hours, committee seats, AGMs, and the routine obligations of co-op membership — as stated in their own words.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant makes specific, concrete pledges (e.g., 'never miss a work day,' 'take on a committee seat,' 'volunteer for the maintenance rotation') and frames co-op duties as personally motivating, not merely acceptable.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant hedges, declines, or explicitly states they will avoid meetings, work parties, and non-essential involvement, or frames participation as unlikely once a financial goal is met.
dimensions.name: Stated Commitment to Active Participation

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the household frames co-op membership as a long-term or permanent home rather than a transitional or financially-motivated stop.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant explicitly describes wanting to age in place, raise a family long-term in the community, or build lasting relationships — no exit plan mentioned.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant explicitly states they plan to move on once they save enough for a down payment or otherwise signals the co-op is a temporary financial solution.
dimensions.name: Long-Term Residency Intent

dimensions.definition: The extent to which the applicant articulates a genuine, intrinsic alignment with co-operative values — mutual aid, democratic governance, shared responsibility, community accountability — as distinct from purely instrumental motivations such as affordability or housing security.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant articulates a substantive personal philosophy of cooperative living — mutual aid, democratic participation, shared stewardship — connecting these values to past behaviour and explicit choices, and describes co-op living as matching how they already try to live.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant's stated motivation is purely cost or generic stability, with no articulation of the co-op model's distinct character; any use of co-op language is hollow or instrumental.
dimensions.name: Co-operative Values Alignment

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the household frames its home life as embedded in shared, communal living — actively seeking neighbourly relationships, informal mutual care, and shared social life — versus preferring a private, self-contained household that happens to share a building.
dimensions.high_end: Household explicitly frames its identity and aspiration around communal living: shared meals, warm neighbourly contact, hosting, performing at care homes, and building genuine relationships as a way of life — sees co-op membership as an expression of this identity.
dimensions.low_end: Household describes itself as preferring privacy and self-sufficiency, keeping to itself, and treating the co-op primarily as a housing arrangement with no particular interest in its social or relational dimensions.
dimensions.name: Communal / Social Orientation Toward Neighbours

dimensions.definition: The breadth and depth of the household's voluntary engagement in community organisations, volunteer roles, and civic life outside the co-op — demonstrating a sustained, demonstrated habit of showing up for others.
dimensions.high_end: One or both adults are actively engaged in multiple external community roles on a regular, recurring basis (e.g., coaching youth sports, running free classes, volunteering as interpreter, performing at care homes, marshalling charity races, repair café participation).
dimensions.low_end: Neither adult describes any current community involvement outside the household; the household presents itself as self-contained with no outward community engagement.
dimensions.name: External Civic & Community Engagement

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the household's lifestyle and expressed values reflect environmental responsibility — composting, waste reduction, sustainable living, food growing — as evidenced by their own descriptions.
dimensions.high_end: The household explicitly describes environmentally oriented habits as part of their everyday identity (growing food, composting, reducing waste, volunteering for recycling, living simply and sharing resources).
dimensions.low_end: No mention of any environmental practice or orientation; the household's essays give no signal of environmental awareness or stewardship habits.
dimensions.name: Environmental Stewardship Orientation

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant's described history and self-characterisation indicate a genuine orientation toward working within collective decision-making processes — patient with slow consensus, willing to disagree without alienating others — rather than taking unilateral action when frustrated.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant describes patience with consensus processes, ability to disagree in a meeting while remaining collegial, and a track record of working constructively within volunteer or governance structures over time.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant self-reports clashing with prior governance bodies, describes other volunteers as disorganised, and implies a pattern of unilateral takeover when dissatisfied with collective pace.
dimensions.name: Collaborative & Consensus Disposition

dimensions.definition: Whether the applicant has a personal, pre-existing relationship with this co-op — through current members, childhood residency, or direct prior visits — giving them grounded, first-hand knowledge of its culture and rhythms.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant has close friends or family already living in the co-op, has spent significant time there, or grew up in this specific co-op, citing that direct familiarity as part of their motivation.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant has no stated connection to this co-op or its members and is applying based on general co-op interest alone.
dimensions.name: Pre-Existing Connection to This Specific Co-op

dimensions.definition: How much free time the household self-reports as realistically available for co-op shifts, committees, and events, accounting for stated outside commitments — distinct from participation intent, which measures willingness rather than capacity.
dimensions.high_end: Applicant explicitly states few or no competing outside commitments and offers to take on more than a standard share of co-op work.
dimensions.low_end: Applicant discloses a heavy schedule of outside commitments (e.g., touring choir most weekends, multiple young children, demanding shift work) that would leave thin actual hours for co-op obligations.
dimensions.name: Discretionary Time Available for Co-op Work

dimensions.definition: The degree to which household income is dependent on a single earner, creating vulnerability if that earner's employment is disrupted — measured as the balance of income between the two adults.
dimensions.high_end: Income is split roughly equally between both adults (neither carries more than ~60% of total), providing a natural hedge against job loss by either earner.
dimensions.low_end: One adult earns the overwhelming majority of household income (e.g., 80%+), meaning any disruption to that earner's employment would severely impact the household's ability to meet housing charges.
dimensions.name: Income Concentration Risk (Dual-Earner Balance)

dimensions.definition: The length of time the household's adult earners have been continuously employed in their current positions, taken together, as a proxy for income reliability and financial predictability.
dimensions.high_end: Both adults have been in their current roles for many years (5+), suggesting low risk of income disruption for either earner.
dimensions.low_end: One or both adults started their current role very recently (within the past 1–2 years) or has a null employment start date, indicating higher income volatility risk.
dimensions.name: Household Employment Tenure & Stability

dimensions.definition: How much financial cushion the household has relative to the co-op's housing charge, as a proxy for ability to absorb unexpected assessments or short-term income disruption without falling into arrears.
dimensions.high_end: Household income is well above the pool median, providing meaningful buffer beyond the housing charge and reducing arrears risk.
dimensions.low_end: Household income is at the lower end of the pool, leaving thinner margin between income and housing costs and less buffer for financial shocks.
dimensions.name: Income Headroom Above Housing Charge

dimensions.definition: Total household income divided by the number of people in the household (adults and children), as a proxy for per-capita financial margin after housing and per-person living costs are covered.
dimensions.high_end: High income per person relative to the pool, indicating that even after covering each household member's costs the household has meaningful financial headroom.
dimensions.low_end: Low income per person relative to the pool, indicating that a larger household is supported by income that may leave limited margin once per-capita costs are fully accounted for.
dimensions.name: Income per Household Member

dimensions.definition: The degree to which the applicant's essays contain specific, verifiable claims about past roles, named actions, and concrete future commitments — allowing the committee to evaluate rather than accept on faith.
dimensions.high_end: Essays name specific roles held (e.g., 'treasurer of a 60-unit co-op, passed three clean audits'), specific tasks performed, and specific contributions planned — leaving little ambiguity about what the co-op actually gains.
dimensions.low_end: Essays consist of very short, generic, or buzzword-heavy statements ('we'll do our share,' 'quick studies and team players') that provide no verifiable detail about skills, experience, or values.
dimensions.name: Essay Specificity & Concreteness of Claims

dimensions.definition: The age distribution of children in the household, reflecting whether children are old enough to participate independently in community life or are at an age requiring intensive adult supervision in shared spaces.
dimensions.high_end: Children are school-age or older (roughly 6+), able to participate in community activities more independently, with lower supervisory burden on parents during co-op activities.
dimensions.low_end: All children are toddlers or infants (under 4), requiring close adult supervision in shared areas, maximising the parental caregiving load during the near term.
dimensions.name: Children's Age Profile for Community Integration

dimensions.definition: The total number of people in the household, which affects wear on shared spaces, demand for common facilities, and the household's social footprint in the community.
dimensions.high_end: A smaller household (2 adults, 0–1 children) places lighter demand on shared infrastructure, common spaces, and building systems.
dimensions.low_end: A larger household (2 adults, 4+ children) places heavier demand on shared spaces, common facilities, laundry, parking, and building systems.
dimensions.name: Household Size & Shared-Resource Demand

dimensions.definition: The number and type of pets the household intends to bring, relevant to allergy risk, noise, shared-space wear, and compliance with co-op pet policies.
dimensions.high_end: Household has no pets, placing no additional burden on shared spaces, allergy management, or pet-related rules.
dimensions.low_end: Household has multiple pets (e.g., both a dog and a cat), placing the maximum pet load represented in this pool on shared outdoor areas and requiring active compliance with pet policies.
dimensions.name: Pet Load

dimensions.definition: The number of distinct co-op functional areas — physical maintenance, financial governance, community programming, digital systems, health/safety, grounds/gardening, new-member onboarding — across which the household offers a named, concrete contribution.
dimensions.high_end: Household offers concrete, named contributions across three or more distinct functional areas, demonstrating versatility that allows the co-op to assign them flexibly across needs.
dimensions.low_end: Household's contribution offer is absent, entirely vague ('do our share of the chores'), or confined to a single narrow function with no secondary offers.
dimensions.name: Breadth of Co-op Contribution Roles Offered
